APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This paper sits squarely within the NLP/ML conference scope: it proposes a practical extension to the anchor-word algorithm (Tandem Anchors) aimed at interactive topic modeling and compares against appropriate interactive baselines (ITM, Utopian, Fast ITM) on standard data (20NEWS) using expected metrics (classification, ARI, coherence). From an experimental-methodology perspective it targets the right audience, but there are minor concerns about reproducibility and evaluation breadth — e.g., no mention of code availability, hyperparameter reporting, and reliance on metadata-derived anchors in high-water-mark experiments — which temper a perfect-fit judgment. | B(4): This paper sits squarely in the interactive topic-modeling subfield and addresses audience interests at a top NLP/ML venue by proposing a user-facing extension (multiword/tandem anchors) to the well-known anchor-word method. The methodology, metrics (coherence, clustering, classification), and comparisons to relevant baselines (ITM, Utopian, Gram-Schmidt anchors) match community expectations, and the emphasis on speed/scalability for interactive use is particularly relevant. I note some practical concerns—evaluation limited to 20NEWS, lack of reported hyperparameters and unclear code availability, and dependence on user/metadata-provided facets—that temper novelty/impact claims but do not remove topical fit for the venue.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper sits squarely within the NLP/ML conference scope: it proposes a practical extension to the anchor-word algorithm (Tandem Anchors) aimed at interactive topic modeling and compares against appropriate interactive baselines (ITM, Utopian, Fast ITM) on standard data (20NEWS) using expected metrics (classification, ARI, coherence). From an experimental-methodology perspective it targets the right audience, but there are minor concerns about reproducibility and evaluation breadth — e.g., no mention of code availability, hyperparameter reporting, and reliance on metadata-derived anchors in high-water-mark experiments — which temper a perfect-fit judgment.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper sits squarely in the interactive topic-modeling subfield and addresses audience interests at a top NLP/ML venue by proposing a user-facing extension (multiword/tandem anchors) to the well-known anchor-word method. The methodology, metrics (coherence, clustering, classification), and comparisons to relevant baselines (ITM, Utopian, Gram-Schmidt anchors) match community expectations, and the emphasis on speed/scalability for interactive use is particularly relevant. I note some practical concerns—evaluation limited to 20NEWS, lack of reported hyperparameters and unclear code availability, and dependence on user/metadata-provided facets—that temper novelty/impact claims but do not remove topical fit for the venue.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper proposes a concrete extension of the anchor-word algorithm—constructing multiword "tandem" anchors via combined co-occurrence pseudowords—and applies it to interactive topic modeling. This is a clear and useful adaptation rather than a fundamentally new paradigm: it builds directly on anchor methods and interactive models (e.g., ITM, Utopian) and the methodological novelty is a non-trivial but fairly incremental engineering/representation change. The authors differentiate their approach against single-word anchors and sampling-based ITMs and demonstrate empirical advantages, so the contribution would be informative to practitioners but would not strongly surprise an expert familiar with anchor-based topic methods. | B(4): The paper introduces a clear extension of the anchor-word paradigm—Tandem Anchors—that systematically constructs multiword anchor facets and combines their co-occurrence profiles into synthetic pseudowords, which is not a trivial re-run of existing single-word anchor methods. This is a meaningful technical innovation that is well-differentiated from prior interactive topic-modeling work (e.g., sampling-based ITM, Utopian) and addresses a real usability gap (more intuitive anchors) while retaining the anchor algorithm’s scalability. While the idea builds directly on the established anchor-word framework rather than creating a wholly new problem formulation, the specific method for forming and using multiword anchors is a non-trivial and useful advance that would inform experts in the field.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper proposes a concrete extension of the anchor-word algorithm—constructing multiword "tandem" anchors via combined co-occurrence pseudowords—and applies it to interactive topic modeling. This is a clear and useful adaptation rather than a fundamentally new paradigm: it builds directly on anchor methods and interactive models (e.g., ITM, Utopian) and the methodological novelty is a non-trivial but fairly incremental engineering/representation change. The authors differentiate their approach against single-word anchors and sampling-based ITMs and demonstrate empirical advantages, so the contribution would be informative to practitioners but would not strongly surprise an expert familiar with anchor-based topic methods.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper introduces a clear extension of the anchor-word paradigm—Tandem Anchors—that systematically constructs multiword anchor facets and combines their co-occurrence profiles into synthetic pseudowords, which is not a trivial re-run of existing single-word anchor methods. This is a meaningful technical innovation that is well-differentiated from prior interactive topic-modeling work (e.g., sampling-based ITM, Utopian) and addresses a real usability gap (more intuitive anchors) while retaining the anchor algorithm’s scalability. While the idea builds directly on the established anchor-word framework rather than creating a wholly new problem formulation, the specific method for forming and using multiword anchors is a non-trivial and useful advance that would inform experts in the field.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper's primary claims are generally supported by empirical comparisons to reasonable baselines (single-word anchors, Interactive Topic Model variants) on 20NEWS and by runtime arguments showing interactive feasibility. The underlying method (forming pseudowords from multiword facets and solving per-word convex KL reconstruction) appears sound, but the summary lacks detail on mathematical proofs and hyperparameter choices, and the workaround for token-level assignments (running LDA with fixed topics) introduces a procedural mismatch that weakens some evaluation claims. Experimental methodology is mostly appropriate, but absence of reported statistical tests, hyperparameters, and code/data for reproducibility are notable minor shortcomings. Overall the contributions are technically credible but would benefit from fuller experimental and reproducibility detail. | B(3): The paper's main claims are supported by experiments comparing tandem anchors to reasonable baselines (single-word anchors, ITM variants) on 20NEWS and by a clear algorithmic construction (pseudowords and convex reconstruction). However, important methodological details are missing from the summary (no hyperparameter reporting, no code, seemingly a single dataset and no mention of statistical tests), and the token-assignment workaround (running LDA with fixed topics) plus reliance on metadata for high-water-mark experiments introduce unsupported assumptions about generality. The mathematical approach (combining co-occurrence rows and convex KL minimization) appears plausible but the summary gives no indication of complete proofs or sensitivity analyses, so while conclusions are promising they are not fully substantiated by the provided evidence.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper's primary claims are generally supported by empirical comparisons to reasonable baselines (single-word anchors, Interactive Topic Model variants) on 20NEWS and by runtime arguments showing interactive feasibility. The underlying method (forming pseudowords from multiword facets and solving per-word convex KL reconstruction) appears sound, but the summary lacks detail on mathematical proofs and hyperparameter choices, and the workaround for token-level assignments (running LDA with fixed topics) introduces a procedural mismatch that weakens some evaluation claims. Experimental methodology is mostly appropriate, but absence of reported statistical tests, hyperparameters, and code/data for reproducibility are notable minor shortcomings. Overall the contributions are technically credible but would benefit from fuller experimental and reproducibility detail.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claims are supported by experiments comparing tandem anchors to reasonable baselines (single-word anchors, ITM variants) on 20NEWS and by a clear algorithmic construction (pseudowords and convex reconstruction). However, important methodological details are missing from the summary (no hyperparameter reporting, no code, seemingly a single dataset and no mention of statistical tests), and the token-assignment workaround (running LDA with fixed topics) plus reliance on metadata for high-water-mark experiments introduce unsupported assumptions about generality. The mathematical approach (combining co-occurrence rows and convex KL minimization) appears plausible but the summary gives no indication of complete proofs or sensitivity analyses, so while conclusions are promising they are not fully substantiated by the provided evidence.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The experiments compare Tandem Anchors to several relevant baselines (single-word Gram-Schmidt anchors, Interactive Topic Model variants, Utopian) on standard metrics (classification, ARI, VI, coherence) but are limited to a single dataset (20NEWS), which weakens claims of generality. Ablations and sensitivity analyses appear limited or unreported (no hyperparameter reporting, and the method relies on metadata-derived high-water-mark anchors without systematic exploration of noisy/realistic user-provided facets). Reproducibility is also a concern: code availability is not mentioned and important implementation/hyperparameter details are missing, though limitations of the approach are candidly discussed. | B(3): The experiments compare Tandem Anchors to several relevant baselines (single-word anchors, Interactive Topic Model, Utopian, Fast ITM) and report classification/clustering and coherence metrics on 20NEWS, which provides reasonable evidence for the main claims. However, the evaluation is limited to a single corpus, lacks reported hyperparameters and code, and does not include thorough ablations or sensitivity analyses for key design choices (e.g., how facet construction or number of words per facet affect results). The paper does acknowledge important limitations (metadata-derived high-water-mark anchors and the need to infer token assignments via LDA), but reproducibility and robustness testing are insufficient to fully support deployment claims for interactive settings.

reviewer_a.justification: The experiments compare Tandem Anchors to several relevant baselines (single-word Gram-Schmidt anchors, Interactive Topic Model variants, Utopian) on standard metrics (classification, ARI, VI, coherence) but are limited to a single dataset (20NEWS), which weakens claims of generality. Ablations and sensitivity analyses appear limited or unreported (no hyperparameter reporting, and the method relies on metadata-derived high-water-mark anchors without systematic exploration of noisy/realistic user-provided facets). Reproducibility is also a concern: code availability is not mentioned and important implementation/hyperparameter details are missing, though limitations of the approach are candidly discussed.

reviewer_b.justification: The experiments compare Tandem Anchors to several relevant baselines (single-word anchors, Interactive Topic Model, Utopian, Fast ITM) and report classification/clustering and coherence metrics on 20NEWS, which provides reasonable evidence for the main claims. However, the evaluation is limited to a single corpus, lacks reported hyperparameters and code, and does not include thorough ablations or sensitivity analyses for key design choices (e.g., how facet construction or number of words per facet affect results). The paper does acknowledge important limitations (metadata-derived high-water-mark anchors and the need to infer token assignments via LDA), but reproducibility and robustness testing are insufficient to fully support deployment claims for interactive settings.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares Tandem Anchors against the main relevant interactive-topic baselines (Interactive Topic Model, Fast ITM, Utopian) and the single-word Gram–Schmidt anchor baseline using standard metrics (classification, clustering, coherence). However, comparisons are limited to a single dataset (20NEWS), hyperparameters and code availability are not reported, and the high-water-mark experiments rely on metadata-derived anchors which may make the setup stronger than realistic interactive scenarios. The authors discuss limitations (metadata reliance and token-assignment workaround) and present runtime and quality comparisons, so overall the empirical comparisons are good but have minor omissions that prevent a fully comprehensive evaluation. | B(4): The paper compares against appropriate and recent baselines: single-word (Gram–Schmidt) anchors, several interactive topic-modeling systems (ITM, Fast ITM, Utopian) and uses standard metrics (classification, ARI, coherence). Comparisons appear fair in metrics and runtime, but rely on a single dataset (20NEWS) and use metadata-derived "high-water-mark" tandem anchors that may overstate practical gains—hyperparameters and code are not reported. The authors acknowledge when prior methods match (similar coherence) and analyze differences in algorithmic approach and speed, so overall the empirical comparisons are strong with some practical omissions.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares Tandem Anchors against the main relevant interactive-topic baselines (Interactive Topic Model, Fast ITM, Utopian) and the single-word Gram–Schmidt anchor baseline using standard metrics (classification, clustering, coherence). However, comparisons are limited to a single dataset (20NEWS), hyperparameters and code availability are not reported, and the high-water-mark experiments rely on metadata-derived anchors which may make the setup stronger than realistic interactive scenarios. The authors discuss limitations (metadata reliance and token-assignment workaround) and present runtime and quality comparisons, so overall the empirical comparisons are good but have minor omissions that prevent a fully comprehensive evaluation.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against appropriate and recent baselines: single-word (Gram–Schmidt) anchors, several interactive topic-modeling systems (ITM, Fast ITM, Utopian) and uses standard metrics (classification, ARI, coherence). Comparisons appear fair in metrics and runtime, but rely on a single dataset (20NEWS) and use metadata-derived "high-water-mark" tandem anchors that may overstate practical gains—hyperparameters and code are not reported. The authors acknowledge when prior methods match (similar coherence) and analyze differences in algorithmic approach and speed, so overall the empirical comparisons are strong with some practical omissions.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper reads well and is well-organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) with the main contributions stated early and the writing quality described as good. The method description (constructing multiword anchor facets, pseudowords, convex reconstruction with KL objective, Bayes’ rule recovery) is communicated clearly, and related work is adequately covered. Minor presentation gaps remain: hyperparameters and code availability are not reported in the summary, and the presence/effectiveness of figures/tables is unclear, which slightly reduces reproducibility and clarity for readers replicating the experiments. | B(4): The paper is well organized with a clear logical flow from introduction through method, experiments, and conclusion, and its main contributions (Tandem Anchors and interactive advantages) are stated early. Notation is consistent and definitions are presented, and figures/tables/examples appear effective for conveying the multiword-anchor construction and experimental comparisons. Writing is generally good and limitations are explicitly discussed, though a few presentation omissions (e.g., hyperparameter reporting and code availability) reduce completeness for readers aiming to reproduce results.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the paper reads well and is well-organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) with the main contributions stated early and the writing quality described as good. The method description (constructing multiword anchor facets, pseudowords, convex reconstruction with KL objective, Bayes’ rule recovery) is communicated clearly, and related work is adequately covered. Minor presentation gaps remain: hyperparameters and code availability are not reported in the summary, and the presence/effectiveness of figures/tables is unclear, which slightly reduces reproducibility and clarity for readers replicating the experiments.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well organized with a clear logical flow from introduction through method, experiments, and conclusion, and its main contributions (Tandem Anchors and interactive advantages) are stated early. Notation is consistent and definitions are presented, and figures/tables/examples appear effective for conveying the multiword-anchor construction and experimental comparisons. Writing is generally good and limitations are explicitly discussed, though a few presentation omissions (e.g., hyperparameter reporting and code availability) reduce completeness for readers aiming to reproduce results.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): This paper addresses a clear practical problem—making anchor-based interactive topic modeling more interpretable and usable—by introducing multiword "tandem anchors" that improve topic quality and remain fast enough for interactive use. The empirical comparisons to multiple interactive baselines (ITM, Utopian, Fast ITM) on 20NEWS and reported gains in classification/clustering and coherence suggest a useful advancement, but reliance on metadata or user-supplied facets, lack of token-level assignments, single-dataset evaluation, and no mention of released code/hyperparameters limit immediate generality and adoption. From an experimental-methodology perspective the idea is promising and likely to be built upon, but its impact is moderate until broader validation and tooling support are demonstrated. | B(4): From an interactive-topic-modeling and HCI perspective, Tandem Anchors addresses an important, practical problem—anchor interpretability—by formally extending anchor-word methods to multiword facets and showing improved classification/clustering metrics while retaining coherence. The reported speed and scalability advantages over sampling-based ITM make it practically useful for interactive workflows, which increases likelihood of adoption. It also opens meaningful research directions around user-supplied facets, metadata-driven anchors, and integrating multiword anchors into token-level inference; however, reliance on facet availability and the need to infer token assignments limit its immediate transformative reach beyond the subfield.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses a clear practical problem—making anchor-based interactive topic modeling more interpretable and usable—by introducing multiword "tandem anchors" that improve topic quality and remain fast enough for interactive use. The empirical comparisons to multiple interactive baselines (ITM, Utopian, Fast ITM) on 20NEWS and reported gains in classification/clustering and coherence suggest a useful advancement, but reliance on metadata or user-supplied facets, lack of token-level assignments, single-dataset evaluation, and no mention of released code/hyperparameters limit immediate generality and adoption. From an experimental-methodology perspective the idea is promising and likely to be built upon, but its impact is moderate until broader validation and tooling support are demonstrated.

reviewer_b.justification: From an interactive-topic-modeling and HCI perspective, Tandem Anchors addresses an important, practical problem—anchor interpretability—by formally extending anchor-word methods to multiword facets and showing improved classification/clustering metrics while retaining coherence. The reported speed and scalability advantages over sampling-based ITM make it practically useful for interactive workflows, which increases likelihood of adoption. It also opens meaningful research directions around user-supplied facets, metadata-driven anchors, and integrating multiword anchors into token-level inference; however, reliance on facet availability and the need to infer token assignments limit its immediate transformative reach beyond the subfield.

main_claims: Tandem Anchors (multiword anchors) extend the anchor-word algorithm to use sets of words as anchors, producing more intuitive anchors for interactive topic modeling.
Tandem Anchors yield higher-quality topics than single-word anchors for interactive topic modeling, improving classification accuracy and clustering metrics while maintaining similar topic coherence.
Tandem Anchors are fast and scalable enough for interactive use, substantially faster than sampling-based interactive topic models (e.g., Interactive Topic Model) and comparable interactive methods.
_summary.methodology: Construct multiword anchor facets and combine their co-occurrence profiles into synthetic pseudowords to augment the original co-occurrence matrix S; solve per-word convex reconstruction problems (minimizing KL divergence against the K pseudoword rows) to obtain coefficients C and recover topic-word distributions via Bayes' rule.
baselines: Fast ITM / optimized Interactive Topic Model (Hu and Boyd-Graber, 2012)
limitations: The high-water-mark experiments use metadata-derived anchors (titles), but the paper explicitly states we cannot assume metadata will be available to build tandem anchors.
The anchor algorithm produces topic-word distributions but not token-level topic assignments (the authors infer token assignments using LDA with fixed topics).
Single-word anchors can be unintuitive; multiword anchors require users or metadata to supply meaningful facets.

reviewer_a.name: Experimental Methodologist
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer / researcher with extensive experience evaluating topic models and building reproducible NLP experiments; has authored papers on evaluation methodology, baselines, and efficient inference techniques.
traits: Insists on strong, well-matched baselines and ablation studies to support empirical claims
Carefully examines experimental design: data splits, hyperparameter selection, and statistical significance
Prioritizes computational reproducibility: runtime, memory profiling, and availability of code/data
Checks fidelity of claimed methods (e.g., correctness of matrix manipulations, optimization objectives) and consistency between algorithm description and implementation
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Detail-oriented and rigorous: focuses on experimental validity, clear baselines/ablations, and reproducibility to ensure empirical claims are well supported.

reviewer_b.name: Interactive Topic Modeling / Impact Specialist
reviewer_b.background: Senior researcher at the intersection of NLP and HCI who studies interactive topic modeling, user-facing topic tools, and the practical adoption of topic models in real-world settings.
traits: Evaluates novelty relative to prior interactive topic-modeling and anchor-word literature, and how the idea advances the field
Assesses practical utility: interpretability, user workflow integration, and whether outputs are meaningfully better for end users
Values clear discussion of limitations, deployment constraints, and broader impact (e.g., biases or misuse in downstream applications)
Looks for evidence that the proposed approach meaningfully shifts the research or application landscape rather than being an incremental engineering tweak
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Contextual and impact-focused: emphasizes novelty, user-utility, and how the method fits into and advances the interactive topic-modeling ecosystem.
